items.q: Why is a cold winter not evidence against climate change?
items.correct: Climate refers to long-term averages, not individual weather events.
items.misconception: It is evidence - if it gets cold sometimes, warming must be wrong.
items.random_wrong: Cold winters are caused by warming.
items.why: Students conflate weather and climate. Climate is the LONG-TERM pattern (decades+); weather is short-term. Global warming refers to average trends - it allows individual cold events even as overall temperatures rise.

items.q: What is the difference between species richness and species evenness?
items.correct: Richness counts how many species exist; evenness measures how balanced their abundances are.
items.random_wrong: Richness is for plants, evenness for animals.
items.why: Students conflate these. A community with 10 species each having 100 individuals has high evenness; one with 10 species but 991 of species A and 1 of each other has low evenness despite identical richness.

items.q: Why does renewable energy require energy storage solutions?
items.correct: Solar and wind generation are intermittent - storage smooths out supply and demand.
items.misconception: Renewable energy can never meet demand.
items.random_wrong: Because they generate too much energy.
items.why: Students dismiss renewables as inadequate. The challenge is intermittency, not insufficiency - the Sun sets, wind drops. Storage (batteries, pumped hydro) addresses timing, not total capacity.

items.q: Norman Borlaug, father of the Green Revolution, won the Nobel Peace Prize in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1970. “1962” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Soil layer containing most organic matter:
items.why: It's O horizon. “B horizon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Borlaug received Nobel Peace Prize?
items.why: The real figure is 1970 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
